questions.question: Define the three-schema architecture and explain the purpose of each schema.
keyPoints: External schema – user‑level view, defines data as seen by specific users or applications.
Conceptual schema – logical level, describes the entire database structure and constraints independent of any application.
Internal schema – physical level, defines how data are actually stored, indexed, and accessed on disk.
The three schemas separate user perception, logical structure, and physical implementation to achieve data independence.

questions.question: Differentiate between logical and physical data independence, highlighting how each is achieved in a DBMS.
keyPoints: Logical data independence – immunity of external schemas to changes in the conceptual schema (e.g., adding/removing attributes).
Physical data independence – immunity of conceptual schema to changes in the internal schema (e.g., new indexing, storage structures).
Achieved by strict separation of levels: external ↔ conceptual ↔ internal, with mapping tables defining relationships.
Allows schema modifications without requiring changes to higher‑level queries or application programs.

questions.question: Explain the external schema (user view) in the three‑schema architecture. Discuss its role in providing data independence, security, and customization, and provide a practical example of an external schema for a university database.
keyPoints: External schema defines a user‑specific or application‑specific view of the database, exposing only the data relevant to that group.
It ensures logical data independence by mapping user queries to the conceptual schema, so changes in the conceptual model do not affect user queries.
Security is enhanced because each external view can restrict access to sensitive attributes or records based on user privileges.

Example: a ‘Student’ external schema may expose fields such as StudentID, Name, EnrolledCourses, and Grades, while a ‘Faculty’ external schema may expose EmployeeID, Name, CoursesTaught, and Salary (the latter hidden from students).

questions.question: Describe the mapping between the three schemas (external‑conceptual and conceptual‑internal). Explain how these mappings facilitate data independence and allow modifications at one level without affecting other levels, providing an example scenario where a change in the internal schema does not affect the external schema.
keyPoints: External‑conceptual mapping translates each external view into a corresponding subset of the conceptual schema, using view definitions or query rewriting rules.
Conceptual‑internal mapping defines how logical entities and attributes are represented in physical storage, including file organization, indexing, and access paths.

Because mappings are stored as metadata, a change in the internal schema (e.g., adding a new index on StudentID) is recorded only in the conceptual‑internal mapping; the conceptual schema and all external schemas remain unchanged.

Example: if a new B‑tree index is created on the StudentID column to improve performance, external queries that retrieve a student’s profile continue to work without modification, demonstrating physical data independence.
